1: hassulammit / machola ha-machanayim: The beloved is here called hassulammit — the only named character in the Song (H7759, appearing only in 6:13 and here). The name is debated: most likely the feminine form of shlomo (Solomon, H8010), meaning she is the feminine counterpart to the king — shlomit, the peaceful/complete one. The LXX reads Soulamitis (the Shunammite), connecting her to Abishag the Shunammite (1 Kings 1:3; 2:17), the young woman brought to comfort David in his old age. Machola ha-machanayim (the dance of two camps/armies, H4246 + H4264) is a hapax; machanayim (two camps, H4264) is the place name where Jacob saw the angel army and called the place Mahanaim (Gen 32:2 — “this is God’s camp”, hence two camps). Whether the dance takes place at Mahanaim, or the beloved dances before two groups, or the descriptor invokes the theophanic camp-vision, the military vocabulary (machaneh = army/camp) applied to the beloved’s dance connects to the “awesome as an army with banners” (6:4, 10) portrait. The wasf (Arabic term for this body-description form) of ch7 ascends from feet upward, unlike ch4’s descending wasf — a structural inversion that creates variety in the book’s praise-poetry.
2: shorer / 'aggan: Shorer (H8326, navel/umbilical cord) is a hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible. The term refers to the navel or the umbilical region. The comparison to an 'aggan (H101, a bowl or basin) that never lacks mixed wine (mezeg, H4197, spiced/mixed wine, also a hapax) is the most anatomically specific metaphor in the wasf sequence. The 'aggan is used for the bronze basins of the sanctuary (Exod 24:6); its application here to the navel invests a bodily feature with the dignity of sacred craftsmanship. The belly (beten, H990) as a mound of wheat ('orem chitta, H6194 + H2406) fenced with lilies (shushanim, H7799) repeats the wheat-fenced-with-lilies image from 7:3 and 4:5, suggesting the beauty of the abundant harvest protected by delicate beauty. The mound-of-wheat image may also echo the harvest-thanksgiving fertility symbolism of Canaanite and broader ANE ceremonial contexts where the body was celebrated as a site of agricultural abundance.
3: The two-fawns twin-gazelle simile for the breasts, repeated nearly verbatim from 4:5, creates a deliberate internal echo within the Song. The repetition is not carelessness but the deliberate intensification of the praise-pattern: the same beautiful image heard again in a new context deepens the picture. The teomei tzeviyyah (H8380 + H6646, twins of a gazelle) describes the symmetry, delicacy, and vitality of the beloved’s body — matching paired features that move together with the elegant coordination of young animals at play.
4: bat-rabbim / hachat-ha-bat-rabbim: The pools of Heshbon at the gate of Bat-Rabbim (H1337, daughter of many) are likely archaeological. Heshbon (modern Tell Hesban in Jordan) was the capital of the Amorite king Sihon and subsequently a Reubenite city (Num 21:25-26; Josh 13:17); excavations have revealed large stone-cut water cisterns near the city. The bat-rabbim gate may be a topographic name for a specific entrance to the city. Eyes as pools emphasize their depth, reflectivity, and stillness — the quality that makes one want to gaze into them. Miqvei cheshbon (H4798 + H2809, the pools of Heshbon) — the miqveh (H4723, gathering of water) root is related to the Jewish ritual bath; the word carries the sense of gathered, standing, clear water. The nose like the tower of Lebanon (migdal hallevano) looking toward Damascus — the great mountain range as the image of a prominent, regal facial feature facing the direction of a powerful city.
5: ha-karmel: The head like Carmel (karmel, H3760) is both a place name (the prominent coastal promontory where Elijah confronted the prophets of Baal, 1 Kings 18) and a common noun meaning a garden-land or fruitful field (used in Isa 10:18, 2 Kings 19:23 for garden-lands; in 2 Kings 4:42 for fresh ears of grain). The double meaning — majestic landmark and fruitful garden — makes the comparison to the beloved’s head capture both her regal bearing and her vitality. The flowing locks described as 'arugah ba-t’chumorot (wavy/bound with purple) — the word arugah or the root behind the translated “dyed/bound with purple” — purple dye was the luxury color of the ancient world, produced from the murex snail by the Phoenician craftsmen. The king captured (asur, H631, bound/held) by the tresses inverts the power dynamic: the king who holds everything is held by the beloved’s hair.
6: The direct address of appreciation — mah-yafet umah-na'amt ahavah bata'anugim (how fair and how pleasant you are, O love, with delights/pleasures) — steps back from the detailed wasf inventory to offer the holistic impression. Ta'anugim (H8588, delights/luxuries) is the noun used in Eccl 2:8 for the sensory pleasures Solomon accumulated; here it is the label for the beloved herself as the embodiment of all delight. The paired adjectives yafeh (beautiful) and na'im (pleasant/agreeable, the root of Jonathan’s no'am and Naomi’s name) describe both visual beauty and the quality of being agreeable, delightful, or at ease — the word that describes Jonathan’s love for David in 2 Sam 1:26 (“your love was wonderful to me”, nifle'ta ahavatkha).
7: tamar: The comparison of the beloved’s stature to a date palm (tamar, H8558) is one of the most evocative images in the Song. The tamar is the date palm — the tree of the ancient Near East most closely associated with royal dignity, fertility, and beauty. Date palms were cultivated throughout Mesopotamia and Canaan; the palm branch is the symbol of victory and majesty (cf. the triumphal palm branches of John 12:13). The name Tamar (given to Judah’s daughter-in-law in Gen 38 and to David’s daughter in 2 Sam 13) means “date palm” — a name of beauty and dignity. The clusters ('eshkolot, H811) of the date palm for the beloved’s breasts use the same word used for the enormous grape cluster brought from the Valley of Eshcol in Num 13:23, suggesting abundance and sweetness.
8: The bridegroom’s declaration that he will climb the palm tree (e'eleh b’tamar, I will ascend the palm tree) and take hold of its branches/clusters (sansinav, H5577, its boughs/date-branches, a hapax in this form) is the boldest statement of intentional pursuit in the Song. E'eleh (I will go up/ascend) is the verb used for going up to Jerusalem, to the temple, and to the high places — it carries connotations of purposeful, upward movement toward something honored or holy. The vine branches whose smell is like an apple (appe-ha-gefen ka-tappuchim) — the fragrance of the vine at close range compared to the apple tree (used elsewhere in the Song as the beloved’s sheltering image, 2:3, 8:5).
9: yayin ha-tov / meyashar ledodi: The mouth of the beloved compared to yayin ha-tov (the best wine, H2896 + H3196) transitions into the beloved’s own voice in mid-verse: “going down smoothly for my beloved” (meyashar ledodi, moving smoothly/pleasantly to my beloved) and “gliding over lips and teeth” (dovev sifatei yeshenim, causing the lips of sleeping ones to speak, or “gliding over the lips of those who sleep”). The yeshanim (H3463, sleeping ones) is interpretively difficult — some understand “the lips of the sleeping” as the wine’s quality of loosening speech even in drowsiness; others (following the LXX lips and teeth) read the mouth imagery as the wine’s pleasant movement across the palate. The transition from bridegroom speaking about the wine-mouth to the beloved taking up the wine image for herself is a grammatical intimacy — the beloved enters the speech as its continuation.
10: teshuqah: Ani ledodi ve'alai teshuqato (I am my beloved’s and his desire is for me) — the mutual-possession formula of 2:16 and 6:3 recurs here with a crucial variation: where 2:16 says “he pastures among the lilies” and 6:3 simply mirrors possession, 7:10 adds teshuqah (H8669, desire/longing). This word occurs only three times in the Hebrew Bible: Gen 3:16 (the woman’s desire for her husband, in the context of the curse — “your desire will be for your husband and he will rule over you”), Gen 4:7 (sin’s desire crouching at the door, waiting for Cain), and here. The theological contrast is profound: in Genesis the teshuqah is bound up with the curse (the distortion of the man-woman relationship through the fall) and with the threat of sin (the appetite that destroys). Here in the Song the same word names the beloved’s desire for the beloved in the context of covenantal love that echoes the pre-fall garden: the teshuqah of the Song is the restoration of the desire that Genesis describes as distorted. The Song does not use the word innocently — it reaches into the curse-vocabulary and redeems it.
11: The beloved’s invitation to go out into the fields (sadeh, H7704) and lodge in the villages (kefar, H3723, villages/hamlets) is the rural-pastoral invitation that runs through the Song as its primary landscape. The word kefar (also the root of Capernaum, kefar nachum, the village of Nahum) describes the smaller settlements around the walled city — the agrarian life of vineyards, flocks, and harvests. The invitation to leave the city and enter the village is the beloved’s initiative — she is the one who leads in this verse, calling the bridegroom into her world rather than waiting for him to call her out.
12: The springtime inventory of the vineyard — nistrah hagefen pitach hasemadar (whether the vine has sprouted, the tender grape blossom has opened) and the pomegranates flowering (heniczu ha-rimmonim) — repeats the spring-survey vocabulary of 2:11-13 in compressed form. The semadar (H5563, the tender grape blossom or grape-bud, appearing in the Song at 2:13, 2:15, and here) is the earliest indicator of the vine’s productive season. Sham 'eten et-dodai lakh (there I will give my love to you) — the beloved’s self-gift offered in the productive landscape of the vineyard; the place of abundance is also the place of self-donation.
13: duda'im: The mandrakes (duda'im, H1736) appear only in two other biblical passages: Gen 30:14-16, where Rachel trades Jacob’s company for Leah’s mandrakes (mandrakes were believed in the ANE to promote fertility and desire, the word related to dod, beloved/uncle/love), and Prov 7:18 (possibly). The Song’s beloved offers the fragrant mandrakes without the competitive anxiety of the Genesis scene — what Leah and Rachel fought over becomes the abundant gift of the beloved who freely gives all her precious fruits. Chadashim gam yeshanim (new and old/new and stored ones) — the image of the beloved who has laid away, over time, all good things for her beloved. The past savings and the fresh harvest, the stored and the new — the comprehensive abundance of a love that has been accumulating its gifts.
